taxonomyTerm.label: 1:1 Oversubscription Ratio (Dedicated)
assumptions: Business requires 100% of their purchased throughput at all times.
constraints: High monthly cost per megabit compared to broadband.
taxonomyTerm.description: The contracted bandwidth is dedicated entirely to the customer, not shared with other subscribers.

taxonomyTerm.label: 5G NR / LTE Connectivity
assumptions: Site is within range of modern upgraded cellular towers.
constraints: Speeds drop drastically if 5G is unavailable and device falls back to 4G/LTE or 3G.
taxonomyTerm.description: Utilizes modern cellular standards to deliver sub-100ms latency and high bandwidth.

taxonomyTerm.label: 802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6
assumptions: Deployed in moderate to high-density environments.
constraints: Clients must have Wi-Fi 6 compatible NICs to realize full benefits.
taxonomyTerm.description: Support for OFDMA and MU-MIMO to handle higher client densities and deliver better throughput.

taxonomyTerm.label: 802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6E
assumptions: Customer requires bleeding-edge performance for AR/VR or extremely dense environments.
constraints: Requires PoE+ or PoE++ power budgets; limited range compared to 2.4/5GHz; requires 6E compatible clients.
taxonomyTerm.description: Utilizes the new 6GHz spectrum for massive bandwidth increases and zero legacy interference.

taxonomyTerm.label: 802.1x Authentication
assumptions: Strict zero-trust or compliance-driven security posture.
constraints: Requires a robust PKI and identity management infrastructure.
taxonomyTerm.description: Requires devices/users to authenticate against a RADIUS server (like Cisco ISE) before gaining network access.

taxonomyTerm.label: Active SIM Card Provisioning
assumptions: Carrier has confirmed coverage at the physical site.
constraints: Tied to specific carrier networks; roaming agreements vary.
taxonomyTerm.description: Physical or eSIM provisioning linking the hardware to a specific carrier data plan.

taxonomyTerm.label: Advanced Monitoring
assumptions: Customer requires proactive monitoring of critical SaaS applications.
constraints: Requires an additional 'Meraki Insight' license tier.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides granular performance tracking for specific web applications and WAN link performance.

taxonomyTerm.label: Advanced QoS
assumptions: Crucial for convergence of voice, video, and data on large campus networks.
constraints: Complex syntax requires specialized engineering knowledge to deploy correctly.
taxonomyTerm.description: ASIC-driven Modular QoS CLI (MQC) supporting deep buffer allocation, strict priority queuing, and shaping.

taxonomyTerm.label: Advanced RF Resource Management
assumptions: Dynamic environments where RF interference changes regularly.
constraints: Aggressive RRM tuning can cause instability if the physical site survey is poor.
taxonomyTerm.description: Cisco RRM dynamically adjusts AP transmit power and channels to mitigate interference and optimize coverage.

taxonomyTerm.label: Advanced Security (AMP, IPS)
assumptions: Customer requires consolidated branch security rather than backhauling traffic to a DC firewall.
constraints: Requires an Advanced Security or SD-WAN Plus license. Reduces overall device throughput.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides intrusion prevention (Snort) and Advanced Malware Protection (AMP) at the network edge.

taxonomyTerm.label: AI-Driven Wireless Analytics
assumptions: Customer desires automated troubleshooting and predictive maintenance.
constraints: Requires Catalyst Center and active telemetry streaming.
taxonomyTerm.description: Uses machine learning to baseline performance and identify anomalous client behavior or RF issues.

taxonomyTerm.label: AI Endpoint Analytics
assumptions: Network contains a large number of headless or IoT devices requiring classification.
constraints: Requires active data telemetry and DNA Advantage licensing.
taxonomyTerm.description: Uses AI/ML via Catalyst Center to automatically identify and profile unknown endpoints (like IoT devices).

taxonomyTerm.label: AI Virtual Agents
assumptions: A significant portion of incoming queries are repetitive and standardizable.
constraints: Requires extensive initial training data and ongoing tuning to prevent customer frustration.
taxonomyTerm.description: Utilizes Natural Language Processing (NLP) to handle routine customer inquiries without human agent intervention.

taxonomyTerm.label: Always-On Scrubbing
assumptions: Customer prioritizes immediate mitigation over marginal latency increases.
constraints: May introduce permanent latency overhead to all inbound connections.
taxonomyTerm.description: All inbound traffic is routed through the provider's scrubbing centers continuously, ensuring immediate mitigation of attacks.

taxonomyTerm.label: API Integration
assumptions: Customer has development resources or automation platforms (like Ansible/Terraform).
constraints: API rate limits apply; complex orchestrations require custom development.
taxonomyTerm.description: REST APIs for automating provisioning, configuration changes, and monitoring.

taxonomyTerm.label: App- and SLA-based Routing Policy
assumptions: Multiple active WAN links are available at the site.
constraints: Policies are globally defined and less granular than traditional policy-based routing (PBR).
taxonomyTerm.description: Routes specific traffic types across the best available WAN link based on latency, jitter, or loss thresholds.

taxonomyTerm.label: Application Recognition
assumptions: The majority of traffic consists of standard, recognizable enterprise and web applications.
constraints: May not correctly identify custom, encrypted, or obscure proprietary applications.
taxonomyTerm.description: Identifies applications traversing the network using deep packet inspection (DPI).

taxonomyTerm.label: Asymmetrical Speeds
assumptions: Traffic profile is heavily skewed towards downloading (typical user browsing).
constraints: Bottlenecks occur when uploading large files to the cloud or hosting on-premise servers.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides high download speeds (e.g., 1Gbps) but significantly lower upload speeds (e.g., 35Mbps).

taxonomyTerm.label: Automated Provisioning
assumptions: Customer values agility and self-service over traditional manual telecom orders.
constraints: Dependent on the carrier's SDN fabric reachability in specific data centers.
taxonomyTerm.description: Allows customers to spin up or adjust interconnections via a web portal or API without manual carrier intervention.

taxonomyTerm.label: Basic Provider-Managed Router/Modem
assumptions: Customer will place their own SD-WAN or Firewall appliance behind it.
constraints: Lacks advanced enterprise routing features; often requires 'Bridge Mode' configuration to use an enterprise firewall.
taxonomyTerm.description: The ISP provides a basic combined modem/router/firewall gateway.

taxonomyTerm.label: Best-Effort Delivery
assumptions: Used primarily as an SD-WAN underlay or for general web browsing.
constraints: Latency and jitter can spike, affecting real-time applications like VoIP.
taxonomyTerm.description: Traffic is routed without strict Quality of Service (QoS) SLAs across the provider network.

taxonomyTerm.label: BGP/OSPF Peering with CE (Customer Edge)
assumptions: Customer networks are large and subnets change dynamically.
constraints: Requires coordination with the carrier for routing changes or prefix list updates.
taxonomyTerm.description: Customer edge router peers dynamically with the Provider Edge (PE) router to advertise local subnets.

taxonomyTerm.label: BGP/OSPF Routing Integration
assumptions: Customer has complex legacy networks or localized data centers requiring dynamic route exchange.
constraints: Requires high networking expertise to prevent routing loops and suboptimal pathing.
taxonomyTerm.description: Full support for redistributing BGP and OSPF routes into the Overlay Management Protocol (OMP) for complex enterprise topologies.

taxonomyTerm.label: BGP Routing Support
assumptions: Customer manages their own public IP space and dual-homes to multiple ISPs.
constraints: Requires an enterprise-grade edge router and an assigned Autonomous System Number (ASN).
taxonomyTerm.description: Allows the customer to peer via eBGP with the provider for dynamic IP failover and multi-homing.

taxonomyTerm.label: CAPWAP Tunneling
assumptions: Customer prefers centralized policy enforcement and IP addressing.
constraints: Can create a bandwidth bottleneck at the controller and requires robust routing from the WLC to the core.
taxonomyTerm.description: Tunnels all wireless client traffic back to the WLC, enabling seamless roaming without VLAN changes at the edge.

taxonomyTerm.label: Cellular Modem/Router
assumptions: Customer needs a standalone cellular bridge to connect to their SD-WAN router.
constraints: Device placement must be optimized for cellular reception, often requiring PoE extension from network closets.
taxonomyTerm.description: Hardware that converts 5G/LTE RF signals into standard Ethernet/IP handoffs.

taxonomyTerm.label: Centralized Software Upgrades
assumptions: Network administrators schedule maintenance windows via the dashboard.
constraints: Lack of granular, localized rollback options; dependent on vendor firmware release schedules.
taxonomyTerm.description: Firmware upgrades are pushed globally or per-network from the Meraki cloud.

taxonomyTerm.label: Cisco DNA Center Integration
assumptions: Customer is deploying a full Cisco SD-Access fabric.
constraints: Requires DNA Advantage licensing and complex SD-Access underlay architecture.
taxonomyTerm.description: Connects the WLC to Catalyst Center to enable SD-Access wireless and centralized policy.

taxonomyTerm.label: Clear Line of Sight to Sky
assumptions: Pre-installation surveys confirm sky visibility.
constraints: Urban canyons or heavily wooded areas will cause frequent micro-outages.
taxonomyTerm.description: The terminal must maintain a wide cone of visibility to the sky to track satellites horizon-to-horizon.

taxonomyTerm.label: Client Monitoring
assumptions: Administrators require user-centric troubleshooting.
constraints: Relies on Layer 7 inspection which may be obscured by MAC randomization and encryption.
taxonomyTerm.description: Tracks individual wireless clients, their OS, application usage, and signal strength over time.

taxonomyTerm.label: Cloud-Based Management -Meraki Dashboard
assumptions: Customer requires simple, scalable wireless management without physical controllers.
constraints: Requires active internet connectivity; APs continue to serve data but cannot be reconfigured if offline.
taxonomyTerm.description: Manages SSIDs, RF profiles, and client tracking via the Meraki cloud portal.

taxonomyTerm.label: Cloud Calling (PBX)
assumptions: Customer is moving away from on-premise PBX hardware.
constraints: Voice quality degrades rapidly if the underlying network has high jitter or packet loss.
taxonomyTerm.description: Full PBX functionality hosted in the cloud, including hunt groups, auto-attendants, and voicemail.

taxonomyTerm.label: Cloud Management - Cisco Meraki Dashboard
assumptions: Out-of-band management access is not strictly required if primary links are stable.
constraints: Requires continuous internet connectivity; cannot be managed locally if the cloud link fails.
taxonomyTerm.description: A unified cloud dashboard providing configuration, monitoring, and troubleshooting tools across the entire Meraki stack.

taxonomyTerm.label: Cloud OnRamp
assumptions: Enterprise relies heavily on public cloud infrastructure.
constraints: Requires specific licensing and integration with third-party cloud APIs.
taxonomyTerm.description: Automates connectivity and optimizes path selection to major public clouds (AWS, Azure, GCP) and SaaS providers (M365).

taxonomyTerm.label: Cloud Provider Interconnect (e.g., ExpressRoute/DirectConnect)
assumptions: Customer hosts mission-critical infrastructure in the public cloud requiring SLA-backed access.
constraints: Requires additional port fees from both the carrier and the cloud provider.
taxonomyTerm.description: Direct, private integration from the MPLS backbone into public cloud providers (AWS, Azure) bypassing the internet.

taxonomyTerm.label: Command Line Interface (CLI) or Catalyst Center Management
assumptions: Customer standardizes on a single management methodology.
constraints: Mixing CLI and Catalyst Center management can cause configuration drift and out-of-sync errors.
taxonomyTerm.description: Can be managed traditionally via local CLI (IOS-XE) or centrally orchestrated via Cisco Catalyst Center.

taxonomyTerm.label: Content Filtering
assumptions: DNS and web traffic traverse the SD-WAN appliance unencrypted or can be categorized via SNI.
constraints: Requires Advanced Security licensing. Less granular than a dedicated Secure Web Gateway (SWG).
taxonomyTerm.description: Blocks access to specific website categories to enforce corporate acceptable use policies.

taxonomyTerm.label: CRM Integration
assumptions: Customer utilizes a modern CRM system (e.g., Salesforce, Zendesk, Dynamics).
constraints: Reliant on the cleanliness of data within the CRM and API limits.
taxonomyTerm.description: Automatically pulls customer records and provides 'screen pops' to agents based on incoming caller ID or interaction data.

taxonomyTerm.label: Custom CTI Integration
assumptions: Business relies on legacy back-office systems that lack modern REST APIs.
constraints: Difficult to upgrade or migrate without rebuilding the custom middleware.
taxonomyTerm.description: Computer Telephony Integration built specifically for localized, proprietary database platforms.

taxonomyTerm.label: DDoS Protection Integration
assumptions: Enterprise is a high-profile target for cyber attacks.
constraints: Adds significant cost; may introduce slight latency during active scrubbing.
taxonomyTerm.description: In-line or on-demand scrubbing of malicious Distributed Denial of Service traffic before it reaches the circuit.

taxonomyTerm.label: Dedicated Control/Management Planes (vSmart, vManage)
assumptions: Customer requires advanced architectural separation for scale and security.
constraints: Requires significant infrastructure to host, or subscriptions to Cisco-hosted controllers.
taxonomyTerm.description: Separates the data forwarding plane from the management (vManage) and control (vSmart) planes for massive scalability.

taxonomyTerm.label: Deep Analytics (vAnalytics)
assumptions: Customer requires proactive intelligence for capacity planning and issue resolution.
constraints: Requires specific DNA Advantage or Premier licensing tiers.
taxonomyTerm.description: SaaS service that ingests telemetry to provide predictive insights, anomaly detection, and long-term trending.

taxonomyTerm.label: DHCP Server
assumptions: Branch networks are small enough that localized, basic DHCP is sufficient.
constraints: Lacks the advanced scope options and redundancy of a dedicated enterprise IPAM/DHCP server.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides dynamic IP addressing to local clients directly from the branch appliance.

taxonomyTerm.label: Direct Routing / Operator Connect
assumptions: Customer requires external calling capabilities inside the Microsoft ecosystem.
constraints: Direct Routing requires managing complex Session Border Controllers (SBCs); Operator Connect limits choice to participating carriers.
taxonomyTerm.description: Methods for connecting native Teams environments to external telecom networks for PSTN access.

taxonomyTerm.label: Diverse Local Access Loops
assumptions: Site is a mission-critical data center or headquarters.
constraints: Extremely expensive and often requires major construction to achieve true physical diversity.
taxonomyTerm.description: Providing two physical circuits from different central offices to the customer site to prevent local outages.

taxonomyTerm.label: Diverse Routing/Dual-Homing
assumptions: Facility is a critical hub or data center.
constraints: Requires expensive construction and specific building geography.
taxonomyTerm.description: Constructing diverse physical fiber entrances into the building to prevent backhoe-fade.

taxonomyTerm.label: Dual-Band Radios
assumptions: Standard enterprise deployment supporting mixed legacy and modern clients.
constraints: 2.4GHz band still requires careful RF planning due to limited non-overlapping channels.
taxonomyTerm.description: APs transmitting simultaneously on 2.4GHz and 5GHz frequencies for maximum compatibility.

taxonomyTerm.label: Dynamic IP Addressing
assumptions: Outbound-only internet access is required.
constraints: Breaks static site-to-site VPNs or inbound server hosting unless DDNS is used.
taxonomyTerm.description: The provider assigns a public IP via DHCP that may change periodically.

taxonomyTerm.label: Event Logging / Syslog - Export
assumptions: Customer utilizes a centralized logging strategy for security compliance.
constraints: Must be configured globally; requires a reachable internal or cloud SIEM.
taxonomyTerm.description: Exports security, flow, and appliance logs to an external Syslog server or SIEM.

taxonomyTerm.label: External Directional Antenna
assumptions: Indoor signal is too weak due to concrete, metal, or low-E glass construction.
constraints: Requires specialized cabling (LMR-400), grounding, and professional installation.
taxonomyTerm.description: Roof or wall-mounted antennas aimed directly at the carrier tower to bypass building attenuation.

taxonomyTerm.label: FHRP (VRRP/HSRP)
assumptions: Network relies on dual distribution/core switches.
constraints: Supported primarily on advanced MS300+ series switches.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides high availability for Layer 3 default gateways using VRRP or equivalent.

taxonomyTerm.label: Fixed-Bandwidth Allocation
assumptions: Workloads demand consistent, unchanging high-volume data transfers.
constraints: Customer pays for idle capacity during low-usage periods.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides a dedicated amount of throughput (e.g., 1Gbps or 10Gbps) that is always available.

taxonomyTerm.label: Forward Error Correction (FEC)
assumptions: Customer utilizes lossy transport (like standard broadband or LTE) for critical voice/video traffic.
constraints: Consumes additional bandwidth (overhead) on the WAN link.
taxonomyTerm.description: Injects parity packets into the data stream to reconstruct dropped packets on the receiving end without retransmission.

taxonomyTerm.label: Guest Access/Portals
assumptions: Corporate security mandates total isolation of guest traffic.
constraints: Complex workflows require Cisco ISE policies and licensing.
taxonomyTerm.description: Integration with Cisco ISE or native WLC portals to provide segmented internet access for guests.

taxonomyTerm.label: Guest Access/Portals - External Portal Integration
assumptions: Customer requires advanced marketing analytics or complex guest workflows.
constraints: Requires ongoing subscriptions to third-party services.
taxonomyTerm.description: Integration with external RADIUS/Captive Portal providers (like Purple or Cloud4Wi) via API.

taxonomyTerm.label: Guest Access/Portals - Internal Portal
assumptions: Customer requires simple, compliant guest access without external servers.
constraints: Customization options are limited to basic HTML/CSS and logo uploads.
taxonomyTerm.description: Meraki-hosted captive portals for guest click-through agreements, SMS auth, or basic billing.

taxonomyTerm.label: High-Performance Enterprise Terminal
assumptions: Customer requires maximum reliability in a remote location.
constraints: Significantly higher hardware cost compared to consumer models.
taxonomyTerm.description: Larger phased array antennas designed for better performance in extreme weather and higher throughput.

taxonomyTerm.label: High Availability
taxonomyTerm.description: testing from script

taxonomyTerm.label: High Availability (N+1)
assumptions: Wireless access is mission-critical to the business.
constraints: Increases hardware and licensing costs.
taxonomyTerm.description: Stateful Switchover (SSO) or N+1 controller designs to ensure wireless networks stay online during hardware failures.

taxonomyTerm.label: High Availability (StackWise/VSS)
assumptions: High availability is mandated at the access or distribution layer.
constraints: Requires specific stacking cables or 10G/40G links; firmware upgrades affect the whole stack.
taxonomyTerm.description: Technologies that allow multiple physical switches to operate as a single logical switch with redundant control planes.

taxonomyTerm.label: Historical Reporting
assumptions: Compliance requirements do not mandate multi-year raw data retention within the dashboard.
constraints: Typically capped at 30 to 90 days depending on the specific metric and licensing.
taxonomyTerm.description: Access to past network performance metrics, client usage, and security events.

taxonomyTerm.label: L2/L3 Routing (OSPF, EIGRP, BGP)
assumptions: Required for a routed-access campus design.
constraints: Advanced routing features require Network Advantage licensing.
taxonomyTerm.description: Full enterprise routing protocol support for complex campus core and distribution layers.

taxonomyTerm.label: Label-Switched Path (LSP)
assumptions: Predictability and stability are prioritized over dynamic pathing.
constraints: Paths are controlled by the carrier, removing granular traffic engineering control from the customer.
taxonomyTerm.description: Traffic is forwarded based on short path labels rather than complex IP routing lookups, ensuring highly predictable paths.

taxonomyTerm.label: Local Syslog & SNMP
assumptions: Integration with legacy IT monitoring tools is required.
constraints: SNMP polling is resource-intensive and not real-time.
taxonomyTerm.description: Standard logging and polling mechanisms for network management systems.

taxonomyTerm.label: Local Voice Gateways
assumptions: Customer maintains dedicated telecom contracts directly to their data centers.
constraints: Vulnerable to local physical outages (power, circuit cuts) unless heavily redundant.
taxonomyTerm.description: On-site routers equipped with DSPs terminating physical carrier circuits (PRI/T1 or local SIP).

taxonomyTerm.label: Low Latency Fabric
assumptions: Applications (like synchronous replication) are highly latency-sensitive.
constraints: Latency is dictated by the physical distance to the cloud on-ramp facility.
taxonomyTerm.description: Traffic is switched across a high-performance Layer 2/Layer 3 fabric specifically engineered for minimal delay between data centers and clouds.

taxonomyTerm.label: Low Latency Orbit Connectivity
assumptions: Allows for functional VoIP and interactive application usage, unlike legacy satellite.
constraints: Latency still spikes during satellite handoffs or heavy network load.
taxonomyTerm.description: Satellites orbit much closer to Earth (approx 550km) than GEO satellites, reducing latency to 40-60ms.

taxonomyTerm.label: Managed CE Router
assumptions: Enterprise prefers a hands-off, fully outsourced WAN routing model.
constraints: Customer has zero access to configure LAN/WAN interfaces or local routing.
taxonomyTerm.description: Carrier owns, monitors, and configures the router residing at the customer premises.

taxonomyTerm.label: Managed Provider Edge Router
assumptions: Customer prefers a managed service model to reduce local IT overhead.
constraints: Customer lacks administrative access to the edge router.
taxonomyTerm.description: The ISP provides, manages, and monitors the edge router delivering the handoff to the customer.

taxonomyTerm.label: Multi-Tenant Controllers
assumptions: Customer accepts a SaaS-based management model.
constraints: Customers cannot dictate controller maintenance windows or host controllers on-premise.
taxonomyTerm.description: Management infrastructure hosted by the vendor and logically partitioned for multiple customers.

taxonomyTerm.label: Multicast Support
assumptions: Customer runs specialized applications that rely on multicast.
constraints: Requires complex configuration (PIM/IGMP) between customer and provider.
taxonomyTerm.description: Carrier backbone supports native multicast routing for applications like live video streaming or financial trading data.

taxonomyTerm.label: Omnichannel Routing
assumptions: Customer's support model extends beyond traditional voice calls.
constraints: Requires agents to be trained across multiple communication mediums simultaneously.
taxonomyTerm.description: Routes voice calls, emails, web chats, and social media interactions to the same agent interface based on skill and availability.

taxonomyTerm.label: On-Demand Mitigation
assumptions: Customer can tolerate brief impact periods at the onset of an attack.
constraints: Detection and diversion take time, meaning initial attack traffic will hit the customer edge.
taxonomyTerm.description: Traffic is diverted to scrubbing centers (via BGP) only when an attack is detected.

taxonomyTerm.label: OSPF v2/v3
assumptions: Campus requires dynamic route learning.
constraints: Requires Advanced licensing and specific hardware models; does not support BGP.
taxonomyTerm.description: Support for Open Shortest Path First dynamic routing for complex campus environments.

taxonomyTerm.label: Out-of-the-box Location Analytics
assumptions: Retail or hospitality environments seeking basic visitor trends.
constraints: Accuracy is limited (often 5-10 meters) and impacted by modern smartphone MAC randomization.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides presence analytics, capture rates, and dwell times based on wireless probe requests.

taxonomyTerm.label: Persistent Workspace Chat
assumptions: Teams prefer asynchronous communication over constant email.
constraints: Can lead to data sprawl and governance issues without strict IT policies.
taxonomyTerm.description: Organizes communication and file sharing into persistent, searchable channels integrated with SharePoint.

taxonomyTerm.label: Pooled Data Plans
assumptions: Enterprise deploys dozens or hundreds of cellular backup connections.
constraints: Requires managing data usage globally to avoid massive fleet-wide overage penalties.
taxonomyTerm.description: Data allowances are shared across all 5G devices in the enterprise fleet to prevent overage charges on single high-use sites.

taxonomyTerm.label: Pre-Provisioned CSP Connections
assumptions: Customer utilizes major, recognized public cloud platforms.
constraints: Limited to the Cloud Service Providers (CSPs) the carrier has pre-integrated with.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provider maintains pre-existing backbone connections to CSPs, allowing customer logical circuits to be provisioned in days rather than months.

taxonomyTerm.label: Premise-Based Call Routing (ACD)
assumptions: Agents are primarily located within physical corporate offices.
constraints: Lacks the agility and rapid feature deployment of cloud alternatives.
taxonomyTerm.description: Hardware-based Automatic Call Distributor routing calls to local IP phones based on pre-programmed logic.

taxonomyTerm.label: Priority Routing/Data Tiers
assumptions: Customer requires consistent performance during peak regional usage hours.
constraints: Throughput is still subject to the physical capacity of the satellite currently overhead.
taxonomyTerm.description: Enterprise plans provide higher priority on the shared satellite network over residential users.

taxonomyTerm.label: Private Carrier Backbone Routing
assumptions: Customer mandates total isolation for security or compliance reasons.
constraints: Sites can only communicate with other sites on the same provider's MPLS network unless complex NNI (Network-to-Network Interfaces) are built.
taxonomyTerm.description: Traffic is routed over a service provider's private infrastructure, completely separated from the public internet.

taxonomyTerm.label: Private Peering (BGP)
assumptions: Customer requires guaranteed privacy and bypassing of public internet transit.
constraints: Requires careful route filtering to prevent cloud routing loops with the enterprise WAN.
taxonomyTerm.description: Establishes private BGP sessions directly into cloud VNet/VPC environments bypassing the public internet.

taxonomyTerm.label: PSTN Integration (Cloud Connected Audio)
assumptions: Customer requires traditional telephony replacement alongside collaboration.
constraints: Requires dedicated telecom porting and regulatory compliance in specific countries.
taxonomyTerm.description: Allows users to make and receive traditional telephone calls natively within the Webex app via Cisco or third-party telecom providers.

taxonomyTerm.label: QOS - Shaping
assumptions: Standard enterprise traffic classes (Voice, Video, Data) fit within predefined capabilities.
constraints: Shaping capabilities are limited compared to complex modular QoS (MQC) on traditional routers.
taxonomyTerm.description: Allocates bandwidth and prioritizes critical application traffic over bulk traffic.

taxonomyTerm.label: QOS - Shaping & Granular Traffic Engineering
assumptions: Customer runs multiple latency-sensitive applications demanding strict prioritization.
constraints: Complex to design and deploy effectively across a diverse WAN fabric.
taxonomyTerm.description: Highly granular QoS capabilities using Modular QoS CLI (MQC) logic, applying advanced queuing and shaping algorithms.

taxonomyTerm.label: Real-time Performance Monitoring
assumptions: Sufficient for daily helpdesk troubleshooting.
constraints: Historical data retention is limited compared to dedicated analytics platforms.
taxonomyTerm.description: Dashboard visibility into channel utilization, interference, and client connection latency.

taxonomyTerm.label: RSTP (Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol - 802.1w)
assumptions: Topologies include redundant Layer 2 links.
constraints: Inferior to modern routed access designs in massive enterprise campuses.
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides fast convergence and loop prevention in redundant Layer 2 topologies.

taxonomyTerm.label: Satellite Dish/Terminal
assumptions: Site location allows for roof or open-ground installation.
constraints: Requires secure mounting, power, and absolute line of sight without tree/building obstruction.
taxonomyTerm.description: Motorized or fixed flat-panel antenna that electronically tracks fast-moving LEO satellites.

taxonomyTerm.label: Scheduled Software Upgrade
assumptions: Maintenance windows align with organizational downtime policies.
constraints: Requires devices to be online during the scheduled window to receive the update.
taxonomyTerm.description: Allows administrators to define specific dates and times for automated firmware application.

taxonomyTerm.label: SD-Access Fabric (LISP/VXLAN)
assumptions: Enterprise requires macro-segmentation independent of physical topologies.
constraints: Requires Cisco Catalyst Center, ISE, and DNA Advantage licensing; complex underlying architecture.
taxonomyTerm.description: Creates a logical overlay network utilizing LISP for the control plane and VXLAN for the data plane, enabling seamless mobility and segmentation.

taxonomyTerm.label: Seamless Roaming
assumptions: Users frequently move throughout the facility while on active calls.
constraints: Requires proper physical overlap between APs (typically 15-20%).
taxonomyTerm.description: Supports 802.11r/k/v for fast transition between access points without dropping voice or video calls.

taxonomyTerm.label: Service-Side NAT
assumptions: Site has overlapping IP space that cannot be easily re-IP'd.
constraints: Can complicate troubleshooting and end-to-end visibility.
taxonomyTerm.description: Translates overlapping internal subnets to unique IP addresses before traversing the VPN.

taxonomyTerm.label: Shared Network Infrastructure
assumptions: Cost savings are prioritized over guaranteed, dedicated bandwidth.
constraints: Throughput drops during peak regional usage times.
taxonomyTerm.description: Bandwidth is shared among multiple local businesses or residential nodes on the same provider equipment.

taxonomyTerm.label: Single-Band Radio
assumptions: Extremely cost-sensitive deployment for low-bandwidth IoT only.
constraints: Severely limits throughput and client capacity; prone to 2.4GHz interference.
taxonomyTerm.description: Entry-level APs providing 2.4GHz or 5GHz connectivity (rare in modern enterprise deployments).

taxonomyTerm.label: SLA-Backed Latency/Delivery
assumptions: Application architecture cannot tolerate variable internet-style performance.
constraints: SLAs are heavily dependent on geographic distance (e.g., transatlantic links inherently have higher latency).
taxonomyTerm.description: Carrier provides strict contractual guarantees for latency, jitter, and packet delivery across the backbone.

taxonomyTerm.label: SNMP
assumptions: Customer relies on legacy monitoring platforms like SolarWinds or PRTG.
constraints: Less efficient than modern streaming telemetry.
taxonomyTerm.description: Support for SNMPv2/v3 for polling interface statistics and receiving traps by legacy NMS tools.

taxonomyTerm.label: Static IP Address
assumptions: Required for building consistent SD-WAN tunnels or hosting local services.
constraints: Usually limits to a single IP or a small /29 block; incurs extra monthly costs.
taxonomyTerm.description: An optional add-on providing a permanent, non-changing public IP address.

taxonomyTerm.label: Static IP Block (/29 or similar)
assumptions: Required for hosting multiple inbound services, DMZs, or SD-WAN edge nodes.
constraints: Customer must justify larger IP blocks to regional internet registries (ARIN/RIPE).
taxonomyTerm.description: Provides a contiguous block of static public IP addresses for enterprise edge routing.

taxonomyTerm.label: Static Routes
assumptions: Routing topology is simple and rarely changes.
constraints: Does not scale dynamically with network topology changes.
taxonomyTerm.description: Ability to define static routes for Layer 3 switching to local subnets or routers.

taxonomyTerm.label: Streaming Telemetry
assumptions: Customer requires instantaneous visibility and proactive assurance.
constraints: Generates massive amounts of data requiring robust collector infrastructure.
taxonomyTerm.description: Pushes continuous, real-time metrics to Catalyst Center or third-party collectors using gRPC/Netconf.

taxonomyTerm.label: Strict QoS Queues (Class of Service)
assumptions: Voice and video applications require absolute protection against jitter and drops.
constraints: Customer must carefully map internal QoS to the carrier's specific Class of Service tiers (usually 3 to 5 classes).
taxonomyTerm.description: Carrier honors QoS tags (DSCP/CoS) across their core, guaranteeing bandwidth for voice and critical data.

taxonomyTerm.label: Strict SLAs (Uptime, Latency, Packet Delivery)
assumptions: Downtime results in immediate financial loss for the business.
constraints: Provider must have extensive infrastructure to offer and honor these SLAs.
taxonomyTerm.description: Service Level Agreements guaranteeing 99.99%+ uptime, specific latency maximums, and financial penalties for failure.

taxonomyTerm.label: Strong IKE and IPSEC Encryption
assumptions: Data traverses untrusted public networks (like the internet).
constraints: High encryption overhead requires appropriate hardware sizing for edge routers.
taxonomyTerm.description: Utilizes standard IPsec with robust cipher suites (AES-256) to secure data in transit across all transports.

taxonomyTerm.label: Strong IKE and IPSEC Encryption (AutoVPN)
assumptions: Devices have unrestricted outbound internet access to the Meraki cloud registry.
constraints: Proprietary to Meraki; does not natively support dynamic multipoint VPN (DMVPN) to non-Meraki peers.
taxonomyTerm.description: Proprietary technology that dynamically builds IPsec tunnels between Meraki sites without manual crypto configuration.

taxonomyTerm.label: Symmetrical Speeds
assumptions: Customer relies heavily on cloud applications, video conferencing, or off-site backups.
constraints: None; highly beneficial for enterprise use.
taxonomyTerm.description: Upload bandwidth matches download bandwidth (e.g., 500Mbps up / 500Mbps down).

taxonomyTerm.label: Tag-based VLANs (IEEE 802.1Q)
assumptions: Endpoints require broadcast domain separation.
constraints: VLAN scaling limits depend on specific MS switch models.
taxonomyTerm.description: Support for standard 802.1Q trunking and access ports for Layer 2 isolation.

taxonomyTerm.label: Teams Phone
assumptions: Customer desires a single-pane-of-glass for chat, video, and voice.
constraints: Advanced contact center routing requires third-party add-ons.
taxonomyTerm.description: Enterprise-grade phone system integration directly into the Teams client.

taxonomyTerm.label: TrustSec Micro-segmentation
assumptions: Customer implements a Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) model.
constraints: Requires comprehensive Cisco ISE deployment and DNA Advantage licensing.
taxonomyTerm.description: Integrates with Cisco ISE to propagate Scalable Group Tags (SGTs) across the SD-WAN fabric for consistent security policies.

taxonomyTerm.label: Usage-Based Billing
assumptions: Cloud workloads have highly variable or bursty traffic patterns.
constraints: Can lead to unpredictable billing if cloud traffic spikes unexpectedly.
taxonomyTerm.description: Pricing model based on the actual amount of data transferred rather than a static bandwidth tier.

taxonomyTerm.label: Vendor Hosted Controllers
assumptions: Customer prefers reduced on-premise infrastructure footprint.
constraints: Removes direct back-end control from the customer; reliant on Cisco SLAs for controller uptime.
taxonomyTerm.description: Cisco hosts and maintains the vManage, vSmart, and vBond components in the cloud (AWS/Azure).

taxonomyTerm.label: Vendor Provided Analytics
assumptions: Sufficient for standard operational visibility without requiring third-party tools.
constraints: Data retention is limited based on the licensing tier; limited raw data export capabilities.
taxonomyTerm.description: Out-of-the-box telemetry providing visibility into client behavior, top applications, and link health.

taxonomyTerm.label: Video Conferencing
assumptions: Endpoints are equipped with cameras and adequate processing power.
constraints: Requires significant downstream and upstream bandwidth at remote sites.
taxonomyTerm.description: Multi-party HD video meetings with screen sharing, whiteboarding, and recording.

taxonomyTerm.label: Volumetric Attack Protection
assumptions: Attacks are intended to overwhelm circuit bandwidth rather than application logic.
constraints: Requires the provider's backbone capacity to far exceed the attack size.
taxonomyTerm.description: Absorbs massive UDP, ICMP, and SYN floods utilizing the provider's massive global bandwidth capacity.

taxonomyTerm.label: Wi-Fi 6E / Wi-Fi 7 Support
assumptions: Deployment in high-capacity venues or for specialized high-bandwidth applications.
constraints: Requires PoE++ (802.3bt) switching infrastructure; range is significantly shorter on 6GHz.
taxonomyTerm.description: Utilizes 6GHz spectrum (6E) and advanced Multi-Link Operation (Wi-Fi 7) for multi-gigabit wireless speeds.

taxonomyTerm.label: Wireless LAN Controller (Catalyst 9800 - Local or Cloud)
assumptions: Customer requires highly deterministic wireless control and scale.
constraints: Requires dedicated hardware or virtual appliances; single point of failure if not deployed in HA.
taxonomyTerm.description: IOS-XE based controller that manages AP configuration, RF policies, and client roaming.

taxonomyTerm.label: Zero-Touch Provisioning (via Catalyst Center)
assumptions: Large-scale campus refresh or expansion.
constraints: Requires pre-configuration in Catalyst Center and DHCP/DNS prerequisites.
taxonomyTerm.description: Plug and Play (PnP) integration allows Catalyst Center to discover and fully provision new switches automatically.

taxonomyTerm.label: Zero-Touch Provisioning (ZTP)
assumptions: Underlying WAN circuit is active and provides basic DHCP.
constraints: Requires a DHCP-assigned IP and unfettered outbound internet access on first boot.
taxonomyTerm.description: Devices automatically download configuration templates upon connecting to the internet.

taxonomyTerm.label: Zero-Touch Provisioning
taxonomyTerm.description: test

catalogItem.name: Business Broadband (fiber)
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Business-class internet connectivity delivered via broadband fiber. Often provides asymmetrical speeds, suitable for non-business-critical primary internet and secondary internet.

catalogItem.name: Dedicated Internet Access (DIA)
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Internet connectivity delivered over a dedicated fiber circuit with a 1:1 oversubscription ratio. Provides uncontended, symmetrical bandwidth directly from the customer premises to the provider's IP backbone.

catalogItem.name: Fixed Wireless Access (5G)
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Wireless internet connectivity utilizing 5G NR and LTE networks. Provides rapid deployment capabilities and physical path diversity from wired terrestrial circuits.

catalogItem.name: LEO Satellite
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Internet connectivity provided via constellations of Low Earth Orbit satellites. Offers lower latency (~40-60ms) compared to GEO satellites but operates over a shared RF medium and shared ground station backhaul.

catalogItem.name: MPLS
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Layer 2.5/Layer 3 VPN technology routing traffic deterministically across a service provider's backbone. Enforces strict Quality of Service (QoS) queues and provides SLA-backed latency and packet delivery guarantees.

catalogItem.name: Meraki WLAN
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Wireless access utilizing Cisco Meraki MR series access points. Features cloud-based centralized management, integrated wireless intrusion prevention (WIPS), out-of-the-box location analytics, and seamless guest access portals.

catalogItem.name: Express connect
catalogItem.detailedDescription: The "Express connect" package is a value-oriented solution for simple network designs, primarily utilizing Meraki SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN components. The SD-WAN features a strict Hub-Spoke network topology with no internal Verizon-provided security inspection, offering Internet breakout options either centrally via the Hub site or locally via a Security Service Edge (SSE) provider. Mandatory SD-WAN features include vendor-hosted, multi-tenant controllers with strong IKE and IPSEC encryption, while standard features cover centralized upgrades, analytics, advanced monitoring, and application-aware routing. The Managed LAN (MLAN) and Wireless LAN (WLAN) components are both cloud-managed and include standard features like Zero-Touch Provisioning (ZTP), centralized software upgrades, and vendor analytics, with the MLAN supporting Tag-based VLANs and basic routing protocols, and the WLAN supporting 802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6 with optional upgrades to Wi-Fi 6e and dual-band radios.
catalogItem.shortDescription: A value-oriented solution designed for simple network designs.

catalogItem.name: Cloud Centric
catalogItem.detailedDescription: The Cloud Centric package, designed for organizations primarily using cloud applications and services, is built on Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN technologies. It outlines a comprehensive network strategy that includes SD-WAN with flexible topology options (Full-Mesh or Hub-Spoke with Dynamic Tunnels), a security model that favors local breakout via Security Service Edge (SSE) like Zscaler or Umbrella, and mandatory cloud-hosted, single-tenant controllers with strong encryption. The Managed LAN (MLAN) and Wireless LAN (WLAN) components focus on standard enterprise features, such as VLANs, RSTP, FHRP, OSPF, and Wi-Fi 6, while offering add-ons like enhanced logging, advanced monitoring, and future-proofing with optional Wi-Fi 6e/7 support, making it a robust, cloud-optimized, and security-conscious network solution.
catalogItem.shortDescription: Designed for organizations where the majority of applications and services are located in the cloud

catalogItem.name: Cisco Catalyst SDWAN
catalogItem.detailedDescription: SD-WAN architecture decoupling control (vSmart), management (vManage), and data planes (Catalyst 8000/vEdge). Supports arbitrary topology creation, forward error correction (FEC), and granular traffic engineering.

catalogItem.name: CCaaS
catalogItem.detailedDescription: A comprehensive cloud-based customer experience solution offering omnichannel routing (voice, chat, email, social), IVR, workforce engagement, and AI-driven analytics without on-premise infrastructure.

catalogItem.name: Cisco Catalyst LAN
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Campus switching utilizing the Cisco Catalyst 9000 series. Integrates with Cisco Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center) to support SD-Access fabric overlays (VXLAN/LISP), TrustSec micro-segmentation, and streaming telemetry.

catalogItem.name: Cisco Catalyst WLAN
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Wireless access utilizing 802.11ax/be (Wi-Fi 6/6E/7) access points and Catalyst 9800 wireless LAN controllers. Implements CAPWAP tunneling, centralized encryption, and advanced RF resource management.

catalogItem.name: DDoS Protection
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Cloud-based or inline scrubbing service designed to detect and mitigate volumetric, protocol, and application-layer DDoS attacks before they impact the enterprise network edge.
catalogItem.shortDescription: Network-level distributed denial of service mitigation.

catalogItem.name: IPCC
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Legacy, premise-based enterprise contact center architecture relying on local servers, voice gateways, and dedicated PSTN circuits (PRIs/SIP trunks).

catalogItem.name: Meraki SDWAN

haEnabled.title: High Availability
haEnabled.description: Deploy redundant appliance at this site

catalogItem.detailedDescription: SD-WAN overlay utilizing Meraki MX appliances. Implements AutoVPN for IPsec tunneling, basic dynamic path selection based on latency/jitter/loss metrics, and unified threat management (UTM) via a single-pane-of-glass dashboard.

catalogItem.name: Meraki LAN
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Layer 2 and basic Layer 3 Ethernet switching via the Cisco Meraki MS series. Features centralized management, topology mapping, and port configuration via a cloud-hosted controller.
catalogItem.shortDescription: Cloud-managed L2/L3 access and aggregation switching.

catalogItem.name: MS Teams
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Cloud-based collaboration platform integrating persistent chat, video meetings, file storage, and enterprise voice (Teams Phone) within the Microsoft 365 ecosystem.
catalogItem.shortDescription: Microsoft's unified communication and workspace platform.

catalogItem.name: Secure Cloud Interconnect (SCI)
catalogItem.detailedDescription: A managed network service providing highly secure, private, and usage-based bandwidth connections from enterprise WANs (like MPLS) directly to major Cloud Service Providers (AWS, Azure, Google).

catalogItem.name: Software Defined Interconnect (SDI)
catalogItem.detailedDescription: An automated, API-driven interconnection service providing guaranteed, fixed-bandwidth private links between enterprise networks, data centers, and Cloud Service Providers via a software-defined fabric.

catalogItem.name: Total Care
catalogItem.shortDescription: Full proactive management and incident response

catalogItem.name: Webex
catalogItem.detailedDescription: Cisco's cloud collaboration platform providing enterprise voice (Webex Calling), video conferencing, messaging, and webinar capabilities delivered as a SaaS application.

itemConstraint.description: Requires Microsoft 365 licensing. Voice capabilities (Direct Routing or Operator Connect) require complex integration with certified Session Border Controllers (SBCs) or telecom operators.
High capital expenditure (CapEx) for hardware and licensing. Difficult to scale rapidly or support remote agents without complex VPN/VDI architectures.
Higher monthly recurring costs compared to broadband. Installation requires dedicated physical fiber build-outs, often resulting in long delivery lead times (30 to 90+ days).
Performance is subject to usage limits depending on the billing model. Requires existing compatible private WAN infrastructure to attach the interconnect.
Fabric provisioning and advanced telemetry require Cisco DNA Advantage licensing and a dedicated Catalyst Center appliance (on-premise or virtual).
Always-on scrubbing can introduce slight latency. On-demand scrubbing requires a traffic diversion trigger (e.g., BGP route injection) which takes time to activate.
Performance is susceptible to RF interference, physical attenuation, and channel overlap. Requires stringent RF site surveys and precise AP placement.

Throughput and tunnel scale are hardware-bound. Advanced security features require specific licensing tiers. Lacks advanced routing protocol manipulation and granular traffic engineering compared to traditional enterprise routers.
Bandwidth is fixed (not usage-based), meaning the customer pays for the allocated capacity regardless of utilization.
Dependent on the quality of the underlying internet or SD-WAN transport. Requires specific licensing tiers for advanced PSTN calling and meeting capacities.
Requires a completely unobstructed line of sight to the sky. Susceptible to atmospheric attenuation (rain fade) and variable throughput depending on satellite passings and regional user density.
Architectural complexity requiring dedicated control plane infrastructure. Requires specialized training for policy creation, troubleshooting, and certificate management.
Requires robust, low-jitter WAN connectivity for agent voice quality. Complex integrations with CRM platforms (e.g., Salesforce) require API development and maintenance.

Delivered over shared infrastructure where performance fluctuates based on localized network congestion, leading to variable 'best-effort' throughput and latency (often lower upload speeds). Lacks stringent Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for uptime, jitter, or packet delivery.
Throughput and latency are variable, modulated by RF signal propagation, atmospheric attenuation, cell tower congestion, and distance to the gNodeB.
Configuration pushes and monitoring require active internet connectivity to the Meraki dashboard. The control plane is cloud-dependent for dynamic updates.
Higher cost per Mbps relative to internet transport. Rigid, static architecture with long provisioning lead times and complex integration requirements for direct-to-cloud (SaaS) traffic flows.

Performance is susceptible to RF interference, physical attenuation, and channel overlap. Control plane, telemetry, and configuration updates require active internet connectivity to the Meraki dashboard.

itemAssumption.description: Customer has adopted the Microsoft 365 ecosystem for identity (Entra ID) and basic productivity tools.
Customer requires absolute data residency, local survivability, or has complex legacy telecom integrations that cannot move to the cloud.
Required for mission-critical sites, large SD-WAN hubs, and environments hosting inbound services where guaranteed throughput and strict SLAs are mandatory.
Customer requires secure, non-internet routing to public cloud resources with flexible, consumption-based billing.
Deployed in environments requiring programmatic network fabrics, scalable segmentation, and high-availability hardware architectures.
Customer has publicly accessible services or IP space that require protection against volumetric attacks.
Underlying wired LAN provides adequate PoE/PoE+ power budgets and sufficient uplink bandwidth to support high-density wireless traffic.
Customer provides underlying IP transport. Out-of-band telemetry and control plane require continuous reachability to the Meraki cloud.
Customer requires highly deterministic, guaranteed capacity for mission-critical cloud workloads (e.g., database replication).
Customer endpoints (PCs, mobile devices, SIP phones) have reliable IP connectivity with appropriate QoS prioritized across the LAN/WAN.
Utilized primarily as an out-of-band management link, diverse backup path, or primary connection in geographic areas lacking terrestrial infrastructure.
Targeted at large-scale deployments requiring complex routing (BGP/OSPF redistribution), strict micro-segmentation, and multi-cloud integration.
Customer seeks to transition legacy on-premise call centers to an OpEx-based, scalable cloud model.
Customer's physical location can be connected to provider fiber infrastructure.
Adequate cellular signal strength (RSRP/RSRQ) is available at the installation point. Often subject to carrier data caps or bandwidth throttling.
Local network topology supports out-of-band management access to the internet. Connected endpoints conform to the switch's available PoE budget.
Required for legacy latency-sensitive applications or core data center interconnects where deterministic routing is an absolute mandate.
Underlying wired LAN provides sufficient PoE/PoE+ power budgets and uplink bandwidth to support high-density wireless traffic. Local topology allows APs to reach the cloud dashboard.

project.name: Acme SD-WAN Rollout
project.rawRequirements: We need an SD-WAN solution with LAN switching and WLAN for 15 branch sites. We prefer Meraki. Cost optimization is important. Requires zero-touch provisioning, application-aware routing for Office 365 and Zoom, and integrated security with automated IPsec tunnels.

projectRecommendation.reason: This package directly addresses all customer requirements: SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN for 15 branch sites. It explicitly states Meraki components, aligns with the vendor preference, and includes zero-touch provisioning, application-aware routing, and integrated security with IPsec tunnels. The 'Max 20 sites' constraint is compatible with the 15 branch sites requirement.

This Meraki SD-WAN service directly matches the SD-WAN requirement, including zero-touch provisioning, application recognition for routing, and strong IPsec encryption. It aligns perfectly with the Meraki vendor preference. However, it only covers SD-WAN and security, not LAN or WLAN.

This package covers SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN, and includes features like ZTP, application recognition, and strong IPsec encryption. However, it is built on Cisco Catalyst technologies, which conflicts with the customer's stated preference for Meraki, significantly reducing its score.

This Meraki LAN service provides cloud-managed switching with Zero-Touch Provisioning, directly addressing the LAN requirement and ZTP. It aligns with the Meraki vendor preference. However, it does not cover SD-WAN, WLAN, or the specific security and application-aware routing needs.

This Meraki WLAN service provides cloud-managed enterprise wireless with Zero-Touch Provisioning, directly addressing the WLAN requirement and ZTP. It aligns with the Meraki vendor preference. However, it does not cover SD-WAN, LAN, or the specific security and application-aware routing needs.

The 'Express connect' package is a strong match as it explicitly includes Meraki SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN, directly addressing the customer's stated vendor preference and technology requirements. Its focus on a value-oriented solution for simple network designs aligns with the 'low-cost' aspect of the SD-WAN requirement, and it covers all requested domains.

Meraki SDWAN directly addresses the core SD-WAN requirement and aligns with the customer's Meraki vendor preference. While it doesn't cover LAN or WLAN, it's a foundational component for the overall solution, especially given the 'Internet-Only Low-Cost Network' context.

The 'Cloud Centric' package covers SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN, which aligns with the customer's technology requirements. However, it is built on Cisco Catalyst technologies, which conflicts with the stated preference for Meraki, significantly reducing its score.

Meraki LAN directly addresses the customer's LAN requirement and aligns with the Meraki vendor preference. It provides cloud-managed switching, which is consistent with a potentially low-cost, simplified management approach.
Meraki WLAN directly addresses the customer's WLAN requirement and aligns with the Meraki vendor preference. Its cloud-managed architecture is suitable for a low-cost, simplified management model.

The 'Express connect' package is a value-oriented solution for simple network designs, aligning well with the 'low-cost' aspect of the requirements. It explicitly uses Meraki SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN components, which directly matches the customer's stated preference for Meraki. Its strict Hub-Spoke topology and Internet breakout options (central or local via SSE) are suitable for an 'Internet-Only' SD-WAN.

Meraki SDWAN is a cloud-managed SD-WAN overlay utilizing Meraki MX appliances, which aligns with the 'Internet-Only' and 'low-cost' aspects due to its simplified management and unified threat management. It directly matches the customer's preference for Meraki. Its features like AutoVPN and dynamic path selection are suitable for SD-WAN requirements.

The 'Cloud Centric' package is built on Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN, LAN, and WLAN technologies, which aligns with the 'Internet-Only' and 'Cloud' aspects of the requirements. While it offers a comprehensive network strategy, it uses Cisco Catalyst, which is a competing vendor to the stated Meraki preference. The package preference rule boosts its score over individual Cisco services.

Meraki LAN is a cloud-managed L2/L3 switching solution, aligning with the 'low-cost' and 'Internet-Only' aspects through its simplified cloud management. It directly matches the customer's preference for Meraki. Its features like ZTP and centralized upgrades are beneficial for streamlined operations.

Meraki WLAN is a cloud-managed enterprise wireless architecture, aligning with the 'low-cost' and 'Internet-Only' aspects through its simplified cloud management. It directly matches the customer's preference for Meraki. Features like ZTP and guest access are relevant for a modern network.
